rulings.Case Title: VERONICA MCCLUSKEY VS. JEFF HENRY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Section 128.7
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTs JEFF HENRY, DAVE WILLNER, and SANAZ EBRAHIMI's Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Section 128.7 Defendants' motion for sanctions pursuant to Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 128.7 is granted. The Court finds that Plaintiff Veronica McCluskey's motion to lift stay, which the Court granted by order dated August 8, 2019, was both factually and legally frivolous. In particular, the Court finds that plaintiff's contention that defendants were in "default" in the arbitration proceedings was entirely lacking in either evidentiary or legal support. In fact, as set forth in the order, and as was fully known to plaintiff's counsel, the American Arbitration Association had made a clerical error by misapplying defendants' timely fees and then, as a result, administratively closed the case. Once the AAA realized and acknowledged its mistake, it requested confirmation from plaintiff that she wanted the case reopened. Plaintiff's counsel did not respond to that repeated request by the AAA, but instead brought the frivolous motion to lift the stay, by which counsel sought to take advantage of the AAA's clerical error and her own lengthy delays in order to evade her contractual obligation to arbitrate her claims and to avoid the effect of the Court's earlier order granting defendants' petition to compel arbitration. Plaintiff's counsel now compounds his misconduct by accusing defendants' counsel of "continued attempts to commit fraud upon this Court and Plaintiff" and of "lying," among other things, accusations which the Court finds to be baseless and unprofessional. Plaintiff's contention that the Court lacks jurisdiction to grant sanctions because it previously granted defendants' motion to compel arbitration is mistaken. (See, e.g., Roberts v. Packard, Packard & Johnson (2013) 217 Cal.App.3d 822, 841 [after granting petition to compel arbitration, trial court retains jurisdiction over the case to conduct further proceedings].) Plaintiff's contention that the definition of a prevailing party in section 128.7 is unconstitutionally vague is unsupported by any authority, and is itself frivolous, as is plaintiff's request for an award of sanctions against defendants for bringing the instant motion. Plaintiff's counsel shall pay defendants' attorneys' fees incurred in opposing the amended motion to lift the stay in the amount of $22,159.50, which the Court finds to be a reasonable amount. The Court does not believe that an award of fees incurred in bringing the initial or the instant sanctions motion is warranted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Turo is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER Z. SILVA VS. CINDY ANN PARDINI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT CINDI PARDINI's MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Off calendar as moot in light of the Court's ruling on the demurrer. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER Z. SILVA VS. CINDY ANN PARDINI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT CINDI PARDINI's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant Cindi Ann Pardini's demurrer to the second amended complaint of Plaintiff Christopher Silva is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff has not cured any of the defects in his prior complaints identified in the Court's orders sustaining demurrers to those complaints with leave to amend. The claims for defamation alleged in Paragraphs 12, 13, 17, and 18 of the SAC are time-barred. (Code Civ. Proc. § 340(c).) Those claims also fail to allege with specificity the words which constituted the alleged defamation, as do the undated (and unauthorized) allegations of slander in paragraphs 15 and 16. The claim for alleged slander in Paragraph 14 of the SAC is barred by the absolute privilege of Civil Code section 47(b). Plaintiff's attempt, without leave of Court, to add a claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress fails to state a claim for an independent tort. (See Felton v. Schaeffer (1991) 229 Cal.App.3d 229, 239 [courts have rejected attempts to avoid the strictures and defenses of libel law merely by artful pleading]; Flynn v. Higham (1983) 149 Cal.App.3d 677, 681-682 ["to allow an independent cause of action for the intentional infliction of emotional distress, based on the same acts which would not support a defamation action, would allow plaintiffs to do indirectly that which they could not do directly. It would also render meaningless any defense of truth or privilege."].) In his "answer" to the demurrer, Plaintiff fails to proffer any intelligible allegations that could overcome the defects in the SAC, and indeed asserts that he intends to file a new lawsuit in Kansas, where he now apparently resides. Accordingly, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: THIERRY HUGAND ET AL VS. ANDRE E. BENGUEREL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT ANDRE BENGUEREL's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendant Andre Benguerel's motion to strike punitive damages from plaintiffs' complaint is granted in part. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the complaint (Complaint, Para. 4) that defendant was skiing downhill "at an unreasonably high rate of speed in a reckless and car[e]less manner" in an area intended for beginning skiers may amount to recklessness but do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [trial court properly granted motion for summary adjudication of punitive damages where no reasonable juror could find that snowboarder's conduct was despicable; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her]; Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) For these reasons, the Court strikes from the complaint the prayer for punitive and exemplary damages, together with the sentence "Defendants [sic] actions were with malice, with knowledge of the injury he was about to cause, in conscious disregard for the safety of Plaintiff." (Complaint, Para. 4.) Plaintiffs have not shown how amendment could cure the defect in the complaint. Accordingly, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. If plaintiffs develop additional facts in discovery that support a claim for punitive damages, they may file a motion for leave to amend the complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ANNE-MARIE WEBER VS. DAVID HESS NULL MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 1, Notice Of Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted; Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion to deem requests for admission, set one, admitted, is granted. The requests for sanctions is granted in the amount of $1,267.50. Plaintiff failed to answer Defendant's Requests for Admission, Set one in a timely fashion. It was not until the day before she served her opposition to this motion that she provided those answers. And those answers are simple denials to each request, preceded by a lengthy "reservation of rights" to change those denials at a later time after the responding party further investigates or otherwise learns something new that may require further response. None of this qualifies as serving substantial responses, nor any of the other circumstances listed under CCP 2033.280. Therefore it is appropriate to deem the requests for admission admitted. The statute makes sanctions mandatory. Prevailing party to submit a proposed order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MENZIES AVIATION (USA) INC. ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Stay Pending Resolution Of Underlying Case.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Stay Pending Resolution Of Underlying Case. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for stay pending resolution of underlying case is granted. "To eliminate the risk of inconsistent factual determinations that could prejudice the insured, a stay of the declaratory relief action pending resolution of the third party suit is appropriate when the coverage question turns on facts to be litigated in the underlying action." (Montrose Chemical Corp. v. Superior Court (1993) 6 Cal.4th 287, 301.) Although the classic case under Montrose is a situation where an insurer can undermine the defense of its insured in an underlying litigation by attacking it on the merits, Montrose is not limited to such a scenario. "If the factual issues to be resolved in the declaratory relief action overlap with issues to be resolved in the underlying litigation, the trial court must stay the declaratory relief action." (Great American Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 221, 235.) "A court considering whether to stay a declaratory relief action must therefore consider precisely which issues are to be litigated in order to resolve the declaratory relief action, and whether those issues are related to factual issues yet to be litigated in the underlying action." (Id.) Here, Plaintiffs' attempt to distinguish the factual issues to be litigated in this action for declaratory relief and in the underlying Tran and Lu v. Javelin et al. action is unpersuasive. In Tran, the claim against Menzies is that it "negligently owned, managed, leased, controlled, and/or operated the land" and "traffic at and around the area" where Mr. Tran was struck. (First Amended Compl., 85.) CCSF's cross-complaint against Menzies for indemnification and defense similarly alleges that Menzies "negligently and/or carelessly conducted" itself in its operations at the airport, leading to Mr. Tran's accident. (Cross-Compl., 7-8.) Moreover, in this action for declaratory relief, Plaintiffs argue that they do not owe the City a defense or indemnification because the Tran incident was not caused by any act or omission by Menzies, Menzies' operations at the airport, or any default by Menzies. Ultimately, the key factual issue in these matters is the same: whether Menzies caused, through its actions or inactions, the Tran accident, or if Menzies is otherwise responsible for that accident. Thus, contrary to Menzies' contention, the declaratory relief action and the Tran action raise overlapping issues, and Menzies does not show that the instant action can be resolved without resolving any fact of consequence in the underlying action. As such, a stay of Plaintiffs' action for declaratory relief is proper and mandatory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MONA GITTHENS ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT NELSON WILLIAMS' MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendant Nelson Williams' motion to strike portions of plaintiffs' complaint is denied without prejudice to defendant raising the issue on a motion for summary adjudication. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(f)(1); see Catalano v. Superior Court (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 91, 92 ["a claim for punitive damages is one of the substantive areas which is properly the subject of a motion for summary adjudication."].) In general, punitive damages may be awarded on a claim for intentional discrimination in an appropriate case. (See Weeks v. Baker & McKenzie (1998) 63 Cal.App.4th 1128, 1147-1148 ["The FEHA does not itself authorize punitive damages. It is, however, settled that California's punitive damages statute, Civil Code section 3294, applies to actions brought under the FEHA, including actions brought for sexual harassment."].) To be sure, not every such action warrants such an award. (See, e.g., Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63-64.) However, the Court does not believe it is a useful exercise at this early stage of the litigation to parse the allegations of the complaint, which purports to state eleven causes of action on behalf of four different plaintiffs, to determine which allegations meet the statutory standard and which do not. Again, defendant may raise the issue later in the case on a complete factual record. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: BRUCE LEE VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, A ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANTs PELIN CINAR and KENZO HIROSE's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants Dr. Cinar and Dr. Hirose's demurrer to the battery claim is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff does not allege he did not give consent to the surgery, but instead argues that his conditional consent was vitiated when defendant physician did not communicate all the information necessary to consent to surgery. (See Compl. 39-40, 63-66.) The allegations do not support a claim for battery against Dr. Cinar and Dr. Hirose because plaintiff does not allege that his consent was predicated upon any condition. When a doctor fails to meet his due care duty to disclose pertinent information, the action should be pleaded in negligence; however, the battery theory should be reserved for those circumstances when a doctor performs an operation to which the patient has not consented. (Cobbs v. Grant (1972) 8 Cal.3d 229, 240-241; see also Ashcraft v. King (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 604, 609 ["The plaintiff's claim of battery rested on the theory that although the operation was consented to, the consent was subject to a specific condition: only family-donated blood would be used. If the plaintiff could establish the existence of this condition and its breach by the physician, she would establish a battery."].) Additionally, the Rains case is distinguishable because plaintiff does not allege the surgery was intended to be nontherapeutic. (See Rains v. Superior Court (1984) 150 Cal.App.3d 933, 941.) Defendant Dr. Cinar's demurrer to the IIED claim is sustained without leave to amend. "Whether a defendant's conduct can reasonably be found to be outrageous is a question of law that must initially be determined by the court; if reasonable persons may differ, it is for the jury to determine whether the conduct was, in fact, outrageous. [Citation.] Ordinarily, a medical diagnosis and treatment advice will not be considered outrageous unless they are false and given in bad faith." (Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518 [sustaining demurrer to intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action where defendant physician urged plaintiff to terminate husband's life support to reduce costs].) In this case, plaintiff fails to plead outrageous conduct as a matter of law. "Liability has been found only where the conduct has been so outrageous in character, and so extreme in degree, as to go beyond all possible bounds of decency, and to be regarded as atrocious, and utterly intolerable in a civilized community." (Cochran v. Cochran (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 488, 496.) Plaintiff does not allege such intolerable conduct and failing to give plaintiff all the information he believed was necessary before consenting to his surgery cannot support this claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JUSTINE MINER VS. SONNY J OLSEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Dismiss Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 11, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANTs KELLY LAWSON and SONNY OLSEN's Motion To Dismiss Complaint Defendants Sonny Olsen and Kelly S. Lawson's motion to dismiss complaint is granted. In the Confirmation and Indemnification Agreement, the parties agreed to a mandatory Utah forum selection clause: "Any party bringing a legal action or proceeding against any other party arising out of or relating to this Agreement must bring the legal action or proceeding in the United States District Court for the District of Utah or in any court of the state of Utah sitting in Salt Lake City." Further, all parties waived any objection "to the laying of venue of any legal action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement brought in any court of the state of Utah sitting in Salt Lake City, or the United States District Court for the District of Utah; and any claim that any action or proceeding brought in any court specified in this section has been brought in an inconvenient forum." Finally, each party submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Utah courts "for the purposes of all legal actions and proceedings arising out of or relating to this Agreement." [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Accordingly, the action is dismissed pursuant to Code of Civ. Proc., §§ 410.30(a) and 418.10(a)(2). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
